content.text: Least-Privilege Secret Access Policies

The principle of least privilege applied to secrets means each workload can read exactly the secrets it needs to function, nothing more. In practice, GenAI services violate this constantly: a single shared Vault token grants the CI job, the prod pod, and the rotation bot access to every secret, or production LLM API keys are readable by any engineer with cluster access.

Implementing least-privilege secret access requires mapping each workload to a distinct identity, writing a policy that allows only specific paths and operations, and adding explicit deny rules for sensitive paths that workload must never reach — even if a future policy change accidentally grants wildcard access.

attrs.alt: Least-privilege secret access matrix for GenAI workloads

content.text: Policy Design Principles

Effective Vault policies follow four rules: path scoping by environment, operation minimization (read-only where write is not needed), explicit deny for sensitive paths, and short TTLs so that a stolen token expires quickly.
Add explicit deny rules for paths the policy should never reach. If a future broad allow-all change is made by mistake, the deny rules override it — Vault evaluates deny before allow.
Kubernetes Workload Identity Auth
In Kubernetes, each pod's ServiceAccount is the identity Vault uses for authentication. Configure Vault's Kubernetes auth method so each ServiceAccount maps to exactly one policy.

# Register Vault Kubernetes auth (run once by platform team)
vault auth enable kubernetes

vault write auth/kubernetes/config \
    kubernetes_host="https://kube-api.internal:6443" \
    kubernetes_ca_cert=@/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt

# Bind genai-service ServiceAccount → genai-service-prod policy
vault write auth/kubernetes/role/genai-service-prod \
    bound_service_account_names=genai-service \
    bound_service_account_namespaces=genai-prod \
    policies=genai-service-prod \
    ttl=1h

# Bind a separate billing-service ServiceAccount → billing policy
vault write auth/kubernetes/role/billing-service-prod \
    bound_service_account_names=billing-service \
    bound_service_account_namespaces=billing-prod \
    policies=billing-service-prod \
    ttl=1h

# k8s/genai-service/serviceaccount.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: genai-service
  namespace: genai-prod
  annotations:
    # Optional: bind to cloud IAM role as well (AWS IRSA / GCP Workload Identity)
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/genai-service-prod
automountServiceAccountToken: true
Scoping by Environment with Namespace Binding

A common mistake is writing a Kubernetes auth role that accepts the ServiceAccount from any namespace. An attacker who compromises a staging pod can then obtain prod secrets. Always bind to both the service account name and the namespace.

# scripts/audit_vault_k8s_roles.py
# Audits Vault Kubernetes auth roles for overly-broad namespace bindings.

import hvac, os, json

VAULT_ADDR = os.environ["VAULT_ADDR"]
VAULT_TOKEN = os.environ["VAULT_TOKEN"]

def audit_k8s_roles() -> list[dict]:
    client = hvac.Client(url=VAULT_ADDR, token=VAULT_TOKEN)
    roles = client.auth.kubernetes.list_roles()["data"]["keys"]
    warnings = []

    for role_name in roles:
        role = client.auth.kubernetes.read_role(role_name)["data"]
        namespaces = role.get("bound_service_account_namespaces", [])
        sa_names = role.get("bound_service_account_names", [])

        if "*" in namespaces:
            warnings.append({
                "role": role_name,
                "issue": "wildcard namespace binding",
                "namespaces": namespaces,
                "service_accounts": sa_names,
                "severity": "CRITICAL",
            })
        elif "*" in sa_names:
            warnings.append({
                "role": role_name,
                "issue": "wildcard service account binding",
                "namespaces": namespaces,
                "service_accounts": sa_names,
                "severity": "HIGH",
            })

    return warnings

if __name__ == "__main__":
    warnings = audit_k8s_roles()
    if warnings:
        print(json.dumps(warnings, indent=2))
        raise SystemExit(f"{len(warnings)} overly-broad role binding(s) found")
    print("All Kubernetes auth roles have explicit namespace bindings.")
Per-Service Policy Files in Version Control

Store every Vault policy as a checked-in HCL file under `vault/policies/`. Apply them via Terraform or a CI job so changes go through code review. Any policy change requires a PR approval from the security team.

vault/
  policies/
    genai-service-prod.hcl
    genai-service-staging.hcl
    genai-ci.hcl
    genai-eval.hcl
    genai-deploy.hcl
    genai-rotation.hcl
    billing-service-prod.hcl
  auth/
    k8s-roles.tf        # Terraform for Kubernetes auth roles
    jwt-roles.tf        # Terraform for GitHub Actions OIDC roles
  apply.sh             # idempotent apply script for CI

#!/bin/bash
# vault/apply.sh — apply all policies from version control
set -euo pipefail

for policy_file in vault/policies/*.hcl; do
    policy_name=$(basename "$policy_file" .hcl)
    echo "Applying policy: $policy_name"
    vault policy write "$policy_name" "$policy_file"
done

echo "All policies applied."
Access Reviews and Policy Drift Detection

Least privilege erodes over time: permissions are added for incidents and never removed, new secrets are added without updating the policy matrix, and service accounts accumulate roles. Run quarterly access reviews and a weekly CI check for policy drift.

# scripts/check_secret_access_matrix.py
# Verifies the actual Vault policies match the declared access matrix.
# Fails CI if any workload can access paths outside its declared scope.

import yaml, hvac, os, sys

MATRIX_PATH = "vault/access-matrix.yaml"   # source of truth
VAULT_ADDR = os.environ["VAULT_ADDR"]
VAULT_TOKEN = os.environ["VAULT_TOKEN"]

def load_matrix(path: str) -> dict:
    with open(path) as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)

def check_policy_paths(client: hvac.Client, policy_name: str,
                        allowed: list[str], denied: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    """Returns list of violations found in the live policy vs matrix."""
    policy_rules = client.sys.read_policy(name=policy_name)["data"]["rules"]
    violations = []

    for denied_path in denied:
        if denied_path in policy_rules and "deny" not in policy_rules:
            violations.append(
                f"Policy '{policy_name}' is missing explicit deny for '{denied_path}'"
            )
    return violations

if __name__ == "__main__":
    matrix = load_matrix(MATRIX_PATH)
    client = hvac.Client(url=VAULT_ADDR, token=VAULT_TOKEN)
    all_violations = []

    for workload in matrix["workloads"]:
        violations = check_policy_paths(
            client,
            policy_name=workload["policy"],
            allowed=workload.get("allowed_paths", []),
            denied=workload.get("denied_paths", []),
        )
        all_violations.extend(violations)

    if all_violations:
        for v in all_violations:
            print(f"VIOLATION: {v}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Access matrix matches live policies.")
Check
Frequency
Blocking?

Yes (CI gate)

Wildcard binding audit

Secret Access Logging and Alerting

Vault emits an audit log entry for every secret read, write, and auth event. For GenAI services the highest-risk events are reads of production LLM API keys — each read should be attributable to an expected workload identity. Unexpected reads (wrong identity, wrong time, wrong frequency) indicate either misconfiguration or compromise.
Testing Policies Before Deployment

Vault's `vault policy fmt` and `vault token capabilities` commands let you verify a policy behaves as expected before applying it to production. Add these checks to CI alongside the policy apply step.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/test_vault_policies.sh
# Run after vault/apply.sh to verify policies grant and deny the right paths.
set -euo pipefail

# Create a temporary token with each policy and verify capabilities
test_policy() {
    local policy=$1
    local expected_allowed=$2
    local expected_denied=$3

    token=$(vault token create -policy="$policy" -ttl=5m -format=json | jq -r .auth.client_token)

    # Check allowed path
    caps=$(VAULT_TOKEN=$token vault token capabilities "$expected_allowed" 2>/dev/null)
    if echo "$caps" | grep -q "deny"; then
        echo "FAIL: $policy should allow $expected_allowed but got: $caps"
        exit 1
    fi

    # Check denied path
    caps=$(VAULT_TOKEN=$token vault token capabilities "$expected_denied" 2>/dev/null)
    if ! echo "$caps" | grep -q "deny"; then
        echo "FAIL: $policy should deny $expected_denied but got: $caps"
        exit 1
    fi

    vault token revoke "$token" > /dev/null
    echo "PASS: $policy"
}

test_policy "genai-ci" \
    "secret/data/genai/staging/openai/api-key" \
    "secret/data/genai/prod/openai/api-key"

test_policy "genai-service-prod" \
    "secret/data/genai/prod/openai/api-key" \
    "secret/data/genai/staging/openai/api-key"

test_policy "genai-eval" \
    "secret/data/genai/eval/openai/api-key" \
    "secret/data/genai/prod/openai/api-key"

echo "All policy capability tests passed."
Run policy tests in CI against a Vault dev server (vault server -dev). This catches capability regressions before the policy reaches production Vault.
Summary
Map each workload (prod pod, CI, eval, deploy, rotation bot, developer) to a distinct Vault identity and policy — never share a single token or role.
Write explicit deny rules for paths each workload must never reach; Vault evaluates deny before allow, protecting against future wildcard grants.
Bind Kubernetes auth roles to both service account name AND namespace — wildcard namespace bindings let staging pods obtain prod secrets.
Store all policy HCL files in version control under vault/policies/ and apply via CI after security-team PR review.
Run weekly policy-drift checks in CI and quarterly human access reviews to prevent least-privilege erosion over time.
For GenAI services, ensure LLM API keys in prod are readable only by the production pod and the deploy job — CI, eval, and developer roles must have explicit denies on those paths.
